focus: Establishing baseline — what the engineer already understands about distributed consensus
action: Assessed existing knowledge — engineer understands leader election at a high level, has used Zookeeper, unfamiliar with Raft specifically
thinking: The engineer is evaluating etcd for a distributed configuration store and needs to understand whether Raft is sound enough to trust in production. This is an applied learning goal — not academic depth, but enough understanding to evaluate operational characteristics and failure modes. Starting from Zookeeper familiarity is useful because it provides a contrast: Zookeeper uses ZAB (Zookeeper Atomic Broadcast), which is conceptually similar to Raft but less readable. I will use the difference to illuminate Raft's design choices rather than explaining Raft in isolation.
insights: Engineer's existing Zookeeper familiarity means I can use ZAB as a contrast reference rather than starting from scratch
Goal is operational confidence, not academic completeness — failure modes are more important than formal proofs

focus: Raft overview — the three core problems it solves
action: Introduced Raft's decomposition: leader election, log replication, safety — explained why understandability was a first-class design goal
thinking: Raft's authors made an explicit choice to prioritize understandability over novelty — this is stated in the paper's abstract and matters for how to teach it. The three-problem decomposition is the right entry point because it maps to what an operator actually cares about: how does the cluster pick a leader, how do writes propagate, and what guarantees that two nodes never disagree about committed state. I avoided formal notation entirely at this stage — the engineer needs a working mental model first, and formalism before intuition creates confusion rather than clarity.
insights: Raft was designed to be understandable — this is why etcd chose it over Paxos, and why the paper is readable
The three-problem decomposition maps directly to operational concerns: leader election = availability, log replication = durability, safety = consistency

focus: Leader election — terms, votes, and the election timeout mechanism
action: Explained election terms, vote requests, and the randomized timeout mechanism that prevents split votes
thinking: The randomized election timeout is Raft's most elegant mechanism and the one most engineers initially find surprising. The intuition — if you randomize when nodes give up waiting and start an election, you avoid the thundering herd of simultaneous elections — is simple but the implications are deep. A node can only vote once per term, which means only one candidate can win a majority, which means split-brain is mathematically impossible within a single term. I used an analogy to a room of people voting for a speaker: if everyone waits a different random amount of time before raising their hand, usually one person raises their hand first and gets elected before others even start campaigning.
insights: Randomized timeouts solve the coordination problem without coordination — an elegant emergent property
The term counter is the clock of the Raft universe — everything that matters is sequenced by term

open.item: What happens when network partition causes simultaneous elections in two partitions?

focus: Log replication — how the leader propagates writes to followers
action: Explained AppendEntries RPC, commit index, and the two-phase write path (write to majority → advance commit index)
thinking: Log replication is where the engineer's confusion typically lives, because there is a gap between 'the leader sends the entry to followers' and 'the entry is safe to apply to the state machine.' The commit index is the bridge — an entry is only committed when a majority has acknowledged it, and only committed entries are applied. I anticipated confusion about the ordering here: entries can be in the log but not committed, and a new leader might have entries that are not committed and need to be overwritten. The two-phase framing (append to majority log → advance commit index) makes the safety invariant visible.

open.item: What happens when network partition causes simultaneous elections in two partitions?
Can a follower's log have entries that are never committed? What happens to them?

focus: Log commitment confusion — the engineer asks why a majority write is not immediately committed
action: Clarified the gap between 'written to majority' and 'committed' — explained that commitment requires the leader to know the majority acknowledged it, and acknowledgment travels back through AppendEntries responses
thinking: This is the most common confusion point in Raft. The engineer assumed that once a majority of nodes have an entry in their log, the entry is committed. This is almost true but not quite — the leader has to receive the acknowledgment responses and advance the commit index before the entry is considered committed. The reason this matters is failure: if the leader crashes before sending the commit index update, followers have the entry in their log but it is not committed, and the next leader can legitimately overwrite it. I used a postal metaphor: writing a letter and mailing it is not the same as the recipient confirming they received it. Commitment is the confirmation, not the mailing.
insights: Commitment is not a property of the entry being in the majority's log — it is a property of the leader knowing the majority has it
This distinction explains why Raft is safe under leader crashes: uncommitted entries can be safely overwritten by a new leader

open.item: What happens when network partition causes simultaneous elections in two partitions?
Can a follower's log have entries that are never committed? What happens to them?

focus: The voting analogy breakthrough — why only nodes with up-to-date logs can become leader
action: Resolved the log divergence confusion using the voting analogy — candidates must prove their log is at least as up-to-date as the voter's log to receive a vote
thinking: The engineer asked: 'what stops a node with a stale log from winning an election and becoming leader?' This is the key safety invariant in Raft and the question that signals the engineer is building genuine understanding rather than surface familiarity. The answer is elegant: a voter will not vote for a candidate whose log is less up-to-date than its own. Up-to-date is defined precisely: higher term wins, same term means longer log wins. This means a majority can only be won by a candidate whose log contains all committed entries — because committed entries were on a majority of nodes, and you cannot win a majority without at least one of those nodes voting for you, and they will only vote for you if your log is at least as current as theirs. The 'voting for the most qualified candidate' analogy clicked for the engineer immediately.
insights: The log completeness invariant is self-enforcing through the vote mechanism — no external coordination needed
A candidate's log being 'at least as up-to-date' as a majority of voters guarantees all committed entries are present
evolution: exploration → convergence: key safety invariant understood through voting analogy

fossils.concept: Intuition that 'majority write = committed' (close but incorrect)
fossils.replaced_by: Committed = leader has received majority acknowledgments and advanced commit index
fossils.reason: The gap between majority write and commitment is what makes Raft safe under leader crashes

focus: Network partition behavior — the split-brain question answered
action: Explained partition behavior — minority partition becomes read-only, majority partition continues operating, reunion resolves via term comparison
thinking: The engineer's open question from turn 3 about split-brain under partition is now answerable with the mental model we have built. If a network partition creates two groups, the minority group cannot elect a leader with a majority vote (by definition — it is a minority). If it already has a leader, that leader will issue writes that no follower acknowledges, and it will not advance its commit index. The minority partition becomes stalled but does not serve stale reads as committed — it simply stops making progress. The majority partition elects a new leader if needed and continues. On reunion, term comparison resolves any conflict: higher term wins, lower-term leader steps down immediately.

open.item: Can a follower's log have entries that are never committed? What happens to them?

focus: Uncommitted log entries and the new leader cleanup process
action: Explained that uncommitted entries on followers are overwritten by new leader's AppendEntries — they are not a safety violation because they were never applied
thinking: The engineer's remaining open question: can follower logs have uncommitted entries, and what happens to them? Yes, absolutely — this is normal. A follower can have entries the previous leader replicated but never committed before crashing. The new leader detects this by comparing log indices and terms during the initial AppendEntries exchange. The new leader's log is authoritative (it won the election by having the most up-to-date log), so conflicting follower entries are overwritten. This is safe because those entries were never committed and therefore never applied to any state machine. No client received a success response for those writes. They are erased as if they never happened.
insights: Uncommitted entries being overwritten is not data loss — it is correction of a write that was never acknowledged to the client
The log is append-only for committed entries; uncommitted entries at the tail are mutable under leader change

focus: Synthesizing the mental model and evaluating etcd's operational characteristics
action: Synthesized Raft understanding into etcd evaluation criteria — identified three operational properties the engineer needs to verify for their use case
thinking: The engineer's goal was to evaluate etcd for a production configuration store. With the Raft mental model in place, I could map the consensus properties to concrete operational concerns. Write availability requires a majority of etcd members to be healthy — a three-node cluster tolerates one failure, five nodes tolerates two. Read consistency depends on whether the client is using linearizable reads (which go through Raft) or serializable reads (which can be served by any node but may be slightly stale). The compaction behavior is the only Raft-specific operational concern that is non-obvious — etcd periodically snapshots state and discards log history, which affects recovery time after a member crash.
decisions: Three-node etcd cluster is sufficient for their use case — tolerates single node failure
Linearizable reads required for their configuration use case — accept the latency cost
Compaction interval tuning is worth reviewing before production rollout

files_out.name: raft-cheatsheet.md
files_out.summary: One-page Raft reference covering: three core mechanisms, term-based sequencing, commitment rules, election safety invariant, partition behavior, and etcd-specific operational notes

focus: Final questions and closing — evaluating readiness to move forward with etcd
action: Engineer confirmed sufficient understanding to proceed with etcd adoption; identified one remaining gap (snapshot/restore procedure) as acceptable to learn operationally
thinking: The engineer reached the right stopping point: enough understanding to make the adoption decision and operate the cluster safely, with a clear view of what they still need to learn (snapshot and restore procedures) which they can acquire through etcd's operational documentation rather than requiring more conceptual work. The cheat sheet captures everything needed to re-orient in a future session without repeating the conceptual build-up. I recommended they run through etcd's disaster recovery documentation before going to production specifically for the snapshot restore path — it is the one Raft implementation detail that is etcd-specific and not derivable from the consensus algorithm alone.

files_out.name: raft-cheatsheet.md
files_out.summary: One-page Raft reference — updated with etcd-specific snapshot and compaction notes from final discussion

files_out.name: etcd-evaluation-notes.md
files_out.summary: Decision record: three-node cluster, linearizable reads, compaction tuning, pre-production snapshot restore drill

open.item: Review etcd snapshot and restore procedure before production rollout

evolution: crystallization: mental model complete, adoption decision made

summary: Built a working mental model of Raft consensus — leader election, log replication, commitment rules, and partition behavior — sufficient to evaluate and operate etcd in production.
